verdict: 2026-07-15 07:28 PDT — The two contract bullets and two presence-only drift assertions are a sound, proportionate design. However, option B’s roadmap shape conflates a review pass with a reviewer backend, and the proposed singleton RFC taxonomy is unnecessary cross-cutting machinery. I would revise those parts before approving the sketch.

findings.title: Option B conflates a pass with a backend
findings.claim: The existing `llm` seam selects an alternative backend for the established design/approach and correctness passes. Option B is instead a new, additional anti-pattern pass intended to run as an independent critic. Calling that pass “the `llm` backend” gives one abstraction two meanings and would make configuration, dispatch, artifacts, and future backend support ambiguous.
findings.alternative: Describe option B as an optional parallel anti-pattern critic pass, separate from backend selection. When built, let the pass use the normal reviewer adapter—or explicitly configure an `llm` provider for that pass—without redefining what the `llm` reviewer backend means.
findings.win: Preserves one backend-selection invariant, avoids a second dispatch meaning and provider-coupled pass design, and allows the focused critic to reuse the eventual `llm` context/schema harness instead of creating parallel orchestration semantics.

findings.title: A new RFC taxonomy is too much structure for one deferred idea
findings.claim: Adding an `RFC-` prefix and a new backlog section for a single optional, evidence-triggered idea expands a backlog convention that currently defines exactly three kinds. That is disproportionate for a story whose implementation is explicitly deferred and conflicts with the repo’s lightweight bias.
findings.alternative: Record option B as `OPS-11`, following the existing `OPS-9` evaluate-and-decide precedent, and state plainly that it is reviewer-tooling evaluation rather than committed work. Revisit the taxonomy only if multiple architectural proposals accumulate.
findings.win: Avoids a new cross-cutting naming convention and section, keeps the change to one backlog item, and preserves the option to introduce a real roadmap taxonomy when more than one item justifies it.
